title: Psalms 73
subtitle: Surely God Is Good to Israel

heb.paragraph: "I envied (qinʾâ) the arrogant when I saw the prosperity of the wicked" (v.3). BOOK III OPENS (Psalms 73–89, mostly Asaph psalms). The Psalter’s greatest wisdom psalm. Asaph nearly lost his faith over the prosperity of the wicked: they have no struggles (v.4), they are free from common burdens (v.5), they scoff and speak with malice (v.8), they say "How would God know?" (v.11). "Surely in vain I have kept my heart pure" (v.13). The crisis: if the wicked prosper and the righteous suffer, what’s the point of obedience?

notes.note: "Surely God is good to Israel, to those who are pure in heart. But as for me, my feet had almost slipped; I had nearly lost my foothold. For I envied the arrogant." The psalm opens with theology (God is good) then immediately confesses doubt (my feet almost slipped). Both are true.

"This is what the wicked are like — always free of care, they go on amassing wealth. Surely in vain I have kept my heart pure." The most honest statement of doubt in the Psalter. Asaph questions the entire project of faithfulness.
"When I tried to understand all this, it troubled me deeply." Human reasoning cannot resolve the problem of unjust prosperity. The mind reaches its limit.

refs.note: Jeremiah's complaint about the prosperity of the wicked addresses the same theodicy question.
Job's extended treatment of wicked prosperity parallels the psalm's observations.
The complaint that it is futile to serve God echoes the psalm's near-stumbling.

hist.context: Psalm 73 opens Book III of the Psalter (Pss 73-89) and the Asaph collection. It addresses the classic wisdom problem of theodicy: why do the wicked prosper while the righteous suffer? The psalmist's near-slip ('my feet had almost slipped,' v.2) confesses how close he came to abandoning faith. The turning point comes 'when I entered the sanctuary' (v.17).

sections.header: Verses 17–28 — “Till I Entered the Sanctuary of God”

heb.paragraph: "Till I entered the sanctuary (miqdashē) of God; then I understood their final destiny" (v.17). The PIVOT. Not new information but new perspective. In God’s presence, Asaph sees what observation alone could not: the wicked stand on slippery ground (v.18), suddenly destroyed (v.19), dismissed like a dream on waking (v.20). Then the confession: "I was senseless and ignorant; I was a brute beast before you" (v.22). And the psalm’s climax: "Yet I am always with you; you hold me by my right hand. You guide me with your counsel, and afterward you will take me into glory" (vv.23–24). "Whom have I in heaven but you? And earth has nothing I desire besides you. My flesh and my heart may fail, but God is the strength of my heart and my portion forever" (vv.25–26).

notes.note: "Till I entered the sanctuary of God; then I understood their final destiny." The turning point of the entire psalm — and arguably the entire Psalter. Worship provides what reason cannot: eschatological perspective.

"Yet I am always with you; you hold me by my right hand. You guide me with your counsel, and afterward you will take me into glory." From near-fall (v.2) to permanent holding (v.23). From doubt to glory. The three-stage promise: present holding, ongoing guidance, future glory.

"Whom have I in heaven but you? And earth has nothing I desire besides you. My flesh and my heart may fail, but God is the strength of my heart and my portion forever." The Psalter’s most complete statement of God-sufficiency. Heaven: only God. Earth: nothing desired besides God. Body failing: God is the portion. Forever.

"But as for me, it is good to be near God. I have made the Sovereign LORD my refuge." The psalm’s final word: nearness to God IS the good. Not God’s gifts but God’s presence is the answer to the prosperity problem.

refs.note: Counting everything as loss compared to knowing Christ echoes the psalm's 'whom have I in heaven but you?'
All things working for good for those who love God develops the psalm's resolved trust.
If the dead are not raised, 'let us eat and drink' articulates what the psalmist almost believed.

hist.context: The resolution comes not through intellectual argument but through worship: entering the sanctuary reveals the wicked's 'final destiny' (v.17). The closing affirmation — 'whom have I in heaven but you?' (v.25) — ranks among the Psalter's most profound statements of devotion. The psalm models moving from envy through worship to renewed trust.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: "Surely God is good to Israel, to those who are pure in heart…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVThey are free from common human burdens; they are not plagued by human ills.ESVThey are not in trouble as others are; they are not stricken like the rest of mankind.NIVThey scoff, and speak with malice; with arrogance they threaten oppression.ESVThey scoff and speak with malice; loftily they threaten oppression.

rows.label: The Crisis — 73:1–16
rows.text: "I envied the arrogant." The wicked prosper; the righteous suffer. "Surely in vain I have kept my heart pure."

hebtext.note: "I envied (qinʾâ) the arrogant when I saw the prosperity of the wicked" (v.3). BOOK III OPENS (Psalms 73–89, mostly Asaph psalms). The Psalter’s greatest wisdom psalm. Asaph nearly lost his faith over the prosperity of the wicked: they have no struggles (v.4), they are free from common burdens (v.5), they scoff and speak with malice (v.8), they say "How would God know?" (v.11). "Surely in vain I have kept my heart pure" (v.13). The crisis: if the wicked prosper and the righteous suffer, what’s the point of obedience?

"Till I entered the sanctuary (miqdashē) of God; then I understood their final destiny" (v.17). The PIVOT. Not new information but new perspective. In God’s presence, Asaph sees what observation alone could not: the wicked stand on slippery ground (v.18), suddenly destroyed (v.19), dismissed like a dream on waking (v.20). Then the confession: "I was senseless and ignorant; I was a brute beast before you" (v.22). And the psalm’s climax: "Yet I am always with you; you hold me by my right hand. You guide me with your counsel, and afterward you will take me into glory" (vv.23–24). "Whom have I in heaven but you? And earth has nothing I desire besides you. My flesh and my heart may fail, but God is the strength of my heart and my portion forever" (vv.25–26).

themes.note: Faith peaks at 10: from near-fall to "my portion forever." Worship: the sanctuary provides what reason cannot. Prophecy: "afterward you will take me into glory" hints at resurrection. Justice: the prosperity problem resolved.
